title: UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION USING VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR BIOMEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION USING VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR BIOMEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Deep learning motivated by convolutional neural networks has been highly successful in a range of medical imaging problems like image classification, image segmentation, image synthesis etc. However for validation and interpretability, not only do we need the predictions made by the model but also how confident it is while making those predictions. This is important in safety critical applications for the people to accept it. In this work, we used an encoder decoder architecture based on variational inference techniques for segmenting brain tumour images. We evaluate our work on the publicly available BRATS dataset using Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and Intersection Over Union (IOU) as the evaluation metrics. Our model is able to segment brain tumours while taking into account both aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty in a principled bayesian manner.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Medical image segmentation is a challenging task for medical practitioners. It is costly, takes time and is prone to error. Hence there is a need to automate the manually done segmentation. Lately Neural Networks have shown great potential on a variety of medical image segmentation problems. The challenge with the approaches used in literature is that the model doesn’t predict the uncertainty associated. This is where Bayesian methods come into play as it gives a principled way of measuring uncertainty from the model predictions. Measuring uncertainty in the output predictions made by neural networks is important for interpretation and validation. Rather than learning the point estimates, Bayesian Neural Networks (BNN) learns the distribution over the weights. The training process of BNN involves first initializing the parameters of the neural network. Next the weights are sampled from some distribution (like gaussian with zero mean and unit variance) and both the forward pass and backward pass is done to update the weights using the conventional backpropagation algorithm.

Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes which still lack theoretical understanding. Bayesian Convolutional Neural Network (Gal and Ghahramani, 2015) use variational inference to learn the posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset. The problem with this approach is that it requires a lot of computation involving a lot of parameters, making this technique not scalable in practice. Variational Autoencoder (Kingma et al., 2015) which is based on generative models solves the above problems and has been successful in a number of tasks like generating images, texts, recommender systems etc. This approach comes with several challenges in its own right which have been successfully tackled in the literature. A random variable sampled from posterior distribution has no gradient so the conventional backpropagation techniques can’t be applied to it. Local Reparameterization Trick (Kingma et al., 2015) was proposed to tackle this by converting the random variable to a deterministic one for computation. The second challenge was the huge computational requirement since it required weight updates in every iteration. Bayes by Backprop algorithm (Blundell et al., 2015) tackled this by calculating gradients in backpropagation using a scale and shift approach by updating the posterior distribution in the backward pass.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

The problem of segmenting medical images have been successfully tackled in literature using mainly two techniques, first using a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) and second those which are based on U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015). The main characteristic of FCN architectures is that it doesn’t use fully connected layers at the end which have been used successfully for image classification problems. U-Net methods on the other hand uses an encoder-decoder architecture with pooling layers in the encoder and upsampling layers in the decoder. Skip connections connect the encoder layers to the decoder layer to create an additional path for the flow of gradients back in the backpropagation step. This helps in reducing overfitting due to many parameters involved while training the network.

## 2.2 BAYESIAN NEURAL NETWORK

Lately, there has been a revival of interest in bayesian methods as some of the inherent problems with deep learning could be solved using it. It is a scalable approach of avoiding overfitting in neural networks and at the same time gives us a measure of uncertainty. This is very important in critical applications where not only do we require the predictions made from the model, but also how confident it is while making its predictions. BNN can be considered as an ensemble of neural networks (Gal, 2016). It has two advantages over the standard neural networks, first it avoids overfitting and second it gives a measure of uncertainty involved.
Instead of point estimates, the neural network learns posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset as defined in Equation 1.
The predictive distribution can be calculated by approximating the integral as defined in Equation 2.

The challenge is that the posterior is often intractable in nature. To combat this, (Neal, 1993) used Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) for learning the weights over the bayesian neural networks. Also (Graves, 2011), (Blundell et al., 2015) and (Louizos and Welling, 2016) proposed independently a technique using variational inference techniques for approximating the posterior distribution. KL Divergence between the posterior and the true distribution can be calculated using Equation 3.
Alternatively minimizing the KL divergence can be written in another form by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) which is tractable. This is shown in Equation 4.

## 2.3 VARIATIONAL INFERENCE

Variational inference finds the parameters of the distribution by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound. ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5.

min KL ( _qθ_ ( _w_ )) _∥p_ ( _w|D_ )) (5)

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The KL divergence is defined in equation 6.
The posterior in the above equation contains an integral which is intractable in nature. The equation can be re written in Equation 7.

The above equation can be decomposed into two parts one of which is the KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation which needs to be minimized and the second is ELBO term which needs to be maximized. This is shown in Equation 8.

KL divergence is zero if exact posterior is equal to variational approximation. Since the KL divergence is always greater than zero hence the equation can be approximated by maximizing only the ELBO (Kingma et al., 2015) as defined in equation 9.

## 2.4 ALEATORIC UNCERTAINTY AND EPISTEMIC UNCERTAINTY

There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty where variance is the sum of both these. Bayesian Neural Networks can be considered an ensemble of neural networks initialized randomly which averages the test results in parallel (Gal, 2016). For final predictions, single mean and variance can be estimated as shown in Equation 10 and Equation 11 respectively.

The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty. Bayesian Neural Network model for uncertainty estimation was done by (Kendall and Gal, 2017) with the last layer representing the mean and variance of logits. The predictive distribution approximating the posterior distribution which gives a measure of uncertainty is defined in Equation 12.

Aleatoric uncertainty is a measure of the variability of the predictions from the dataset hence it is inherent in the data present. The aleatoric uncertainty is the uncertainty that exists inside the dataset. It captures noise inherent in the observations, which arises from the distribution of data. It is highly dependent on bias and distribution inside the input data but not the size of training samples.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Epistemic uncertainty on the other hand is a measure of the variability of predictions from the model which is tied to various metrics used for evaluation like accuracy, loss etc. It represents the uncertainty inside the model. The epistemic uncertainty will decrease with enough training samples. It is resulting from the limitation of knowledge and data of the system.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 DATASET

To validate the performance of our proposed approach to generalization, publicly available datasets were used for brain tumour segmentation BRATS18 (Menze et al., 2015) and (Bakas et al., 2018). It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma. The images were of resolution 240 _×_ 240 _×_ 155 pixels. The ground truth labels were created by expert neuroradiologists. The sample from the dataset is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1: Example of MRI slices and ground truth segmentation

## 3.2 DATA AUGMENTATION

The following data augmentation methods were used to increase the size of dataset:

**1. Rescaling:** We rescale the pixels values by rescaling factor 1/255.

**2. Rotation:** Random rotations with the setup degree range between [0, 360] were used.
**3. Height and Width Shift:** Shifting the input to the left or right and up or down was performed.
**4. Shearing Intensity:** It refers to shear angle (unit in degrees) in counter-clockwise direction.

**5. Brightness:** It uses a brightness shift value from the setup range.

## 3.3 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The prior distribution helps to incorporate learning of the weights over the network. Variational Autoencoder has been used successively as a kind of generative model by sampling from the prior distribution in the encoder. The decoder uses the mean vector and standard deviation vector from the latent space to reconstruct the input. Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture. We tried different backbones which have previously enjoyed success and found original U-Net gave the best results. The input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, the standard deviation vector of the conditional distribution expressing the confidence with which the pixels are correctly predicted. After passing through the encoder, the parameters get converted to a latent representation which is again sampled in a mean and standard deviation vector. The decoder later recovers this back to the original distribution. The conventional backpropagation algorithm is used for training the model with gradient descent. The network architecture is shown in Figure 1.
Let dataset be denoted by _D_ : _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}[N] i_ =1[, variational approximation of the posterior distribution] by _qθ_ ( _w_ ), encoder as _rψ_ ( _z|w_ ) and decoder as _pφ_ ( _w_ ).

The objective function used in this work is defined in Equation 13:

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 2: Illustration of our network architecture

The network is trained using Gradient Descent as defined in Equation 14 and Equation 15:
We need as predictions the posterior distribution of the model parameters which is denoted as _qθ_ ( _w_ ).

The complete algorithm used in our work is shown below:

**Algorithm 1:** Uncertainty Quantification using Variational Inference for Biomedical Image Segmentation

Input: Dataset _D_ : _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}[N] i_ =1 Input: Variational approximation of the posterior distribution _qθ_ ( _w_ ) Input: encoder _rψ_ ( _z|w_ ) and decoder _pφ_ ( _w_ ) **while** _not converged_ **do**

Sample minibatch: _D[∗]_ : _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}[M] i_ =1 � Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_ � _w_[(] _[i]_[)][�] � Sample latent variables with reparametrization: _z_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ rψ_ ( _i_ ) � _z|w_ �[(] _[i]_[)][�] Compute stochastic gradients of the objective: � � � � _L_[approx] =[�] _i_ � _−_ log _qθi_ � _w_[(] _[i]_[)][��] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ ) � _z|w_ �[(] _[i]_[)][�] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ ) � _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_ �� Update parameters _θ_ = _θ_ + _α∇θL_ and _ψ_ = _ψ_ + _β∇ψL gφ ← m_[1] � _mk_ =1 _[∇][φ]_[ log] _[ p][φ]_ � _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _|zθ_ � _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _, ϵ_[(] _[k]_[)][��] **end** Output: _qθ_ ( _w_ ) _−_ posterior distribution of the model parameters

## 3.4 EXPERIMENTAL DETAILS

The hyperparameters used in our model are specified in Table 1.

In addition to the above hyperparamaters, cyclical learning rate schedulers and ReduceLROnPlateau was used. In gradient descent, the value of momentum was taken as 0.9, _γ_ value of 0.1 and weight decay of 0.0005.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 1: Hyperparameters details

|Parameter|Value|
|---|---|
|Batch Size|16|
|Optimizer|Adam|
|Learning Rate|0.001|
|LR scheduler patience|10|
|LR scheduler factor|0.1|
|Latent Variable Size|10|
|Max epochs|500|
|Dropout(Both Training and Test)|0.3|

## 3.5 LOSS FUNCTIONS

A combination of binary cross entropy and dice losses have been used to train the network. The first part binary cross entropy is a commonly used loss function for classification problems as shown by (Goodfellow et al., 2016). Every pixel in the image needs to be classified and hence loss function can be written as shown in Equation 18.

The problem with binary cross entropy loss is that it doesn’t take into account the class imbalance as the background is the dominant class. This is one of fundamental challenges in semantic segmentation problems. Dice Loss is robust to the aforementioned problem which is based on Dice Similarity Coefficient as defined in Equation 19.
Both the loss terms were combined in a single term with more weight given to the Dice Loss term since it handles the class imbalance problem better. This is shown in Equation 20.

## 3.6 EVALUATION METRICS

Evaluation metrics for semantic segmentation problems which have often been used in literature are Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) also known as F1-score and Intersection over union (IoU). The corresponding equations are shown in Equation 16 and Equation 17 respectively.

True positive (TR), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP) number of pixels is calculated separately for each image and averaged over the test set. The ground truth is labelled manually by experts which are compared against.

## 4 RESULTS

Figure 3 displays how accuracy (y-axis) behaves based on how much the data (x-axis) holds.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 3: Accuracy vs data distribution of our network using uncertainty quantification approach on validation/test sets.
We have to generate the uncertainty value of each input based on the predicted output. This can be done using the below uncertainty quantification methods:
**1. MC dropout:** This is a method based on Bayesian Neural Networks and activated dropout function at inference time. This can be defined using the below equation:
where _M_ is MC sample size, _c_ is the number of classes, _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _M_ , _c_ and _pi_ is the probability distribution of each image.

**2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.

**3. Ensemble MC dropout:** This method is a combination of MC dropout and deep ensembles. It contains 5 Bayesian Neural Networks and thus following a similar way to compute MC dropout but with the average value of these five models. This can be defined using the below equation:
where _D_ presents how many models are there in ensemble MC dropout, _k_ , _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _D_ , _M_ , _c_ and other variables same as MC dropout uncertainty formula above.

Table 2: Uncertainty mean value of samples in the validation/test sets by our network.

|Model|Uncertainty(Val)|Uncertainty(Test)|
|---|---|---|
|MC dropout|0.1607|0.1217|
|Deep ensembles|0.1204|0.1146|
|Ensemble MC dropout|0.2235|0.1213|
The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.
The IOU value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 3.

The predicted segmentation along with uncertainty involved in segmentation is shown in Figure 4:

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 3: Mean Dice Similarity metrics for the experiments

|Train Size|DSC|
|---|---|
|5|53.1|
|10|56.6|
|15|60.8|
|20|64.3|

Table 4: Intersection over Union metrics for the experiments

|Train Size|IOU|
|---|---|
|5|48.4|
|10|50.6|
|15|53.1|
|20|55.8|

Figure 4: Examples of models predictions on test samples, compared to ground truth segmentation. First column: input image, second column: ground truth segmentation, third column: predicted segmentation, fourth column: aleatoric uncertainty and fifth column: epistemic uncertainty.

The darker(dull) color denotes more confidence while the lighter(brighter) color means the model is less confident while segmenting those areas. Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we proposed a way to quantify uncertainty in the context of medical image segmentation. Our network is based on an encoder decoder framework similar to that used by VAEs. The weights of the network represent distributions instead of point estimates and thus give a principled way of measuring uncertainty at the same time while making the predictions. Our model uses bayesian neural networks for both the encoder and decoder. The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly. We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We would also like to thank Nvidia for providing the GPUs.

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important topic in medical imaging—uncertainty quantification—but suffers from significant technical contradictions, vague methodological descriptions, and potential errors in the reported results. While the authors successfully identify the two main types of uncertainty (aleatoric and epistemic), the formal description of their architecture fluctuates between a standard Bayesian Neural Network and a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) without clarifying how these two distinct frameworks are integrated for a segmentation task. Furthermore, the quantitative results presented in the tables are unusually low for the BRATS dataset (Dice scores in the 50s-60s), and the algorithm block contains confusing notation and undefined variables that hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradiction in ELBO definition
comments.quote: ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5. min KL ( _qθ_ ( _w_ )) _∥p_ ( _w|D_ )) (5)
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the text states that the ELBO is the sum of KL divergence and negative log-likelihood. However, the standard definition of the Evidence Lower Bound is the log-likelihood minus the KL divergence (or NLL plus KL if minimizing the negative ELBO). More importantly, Equation 5 is labeled as the definition of ELBO but actually shows the minimization of the KL divergence between the variational distribution and the true posterior. These are related concepts but not identical, and the sign convention (minimizing vs maximizing) is inconsistent between the text and the formulas.

comments.title: Architectural ambiguity and input inconsistency
comments.quote: The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly.
comments.explanation: The authors describe a VAE-like structure where the 'encoder' takes input from a pre-trained segmentation network. It is unclear if the encoder is processing the raw image, the logits of the pre-trained model, or the softmax probabilities. Furthermore, if the weights $w$ are being learned as distributions (as per BNN theory in Section 2.2), it is unclear why a VAE latent space $z$ is introduced in Equation 13 and Algorithm 1. The relationship between the network weights $w$ and the latent variable $z$ is not standard in BNN for segmentation and is underspecified here.

comments.title: Erroneous Algorithm Logic
comments.quote: $gφ \leftarrow m_{[1]} \sum mk=1 [\nabla][φ][ log] [ p][φ] ( x[( k)] |zθ x[( k)] , \epsilon[( k)] )$
comments.explanation: The update rule for $\phi$ in Algorithm 1 appears to be a garbled version of a VAE gradient estimator. It references variables like $x$ and $\epsilon$ which are not defined in the algorithm's input list. Furthermore, the summation index $mk=1$ and the structure of the gradient $g_\phi$ do not follow standard backpropagation or VI notation. The use of $z_\theta$ as a subscript is also inconsistent with the earlier definition of $\phi$ as the decoder parameters and $\theta$ as the variational parameters.

comments.title: Anomalously low performance metrics
comments.quote: Table 3: Mean Dice Similarity metrics for the experiments... 20 | 64.3
comments.explanation: The BRATS dataset is a well-established benchmark where state-of-the-art methods typically achieve Dice Similarity Coefficients (DSC) in the range of 0.85 to 0.92 for the whole tumor. The authors report a maximum DSC of 64.3 (ostensibly 0.643). While they mention 'Train Size' values of 5, 10, 15, and 20, it is unclear if these refer to the number of patients, percent of data, or something else. If the model only achieves ~64% DSC on a dataset where standard U-Nets exceed 85%, the 'state of the art' claim in the conclusion is mathematically contradicted by the provided tables.

comments.title: Confusing Equation for Predictive Distribution
comments.quote: $p(y|x, D) \approx \int p(y|x, w) q_\theta(w) dw$ (Equation 12)
comments.explanation: Equation 12 is presented as the 'predictive distribution approximating the posterior distribution'. In Bayesian statistics, the posterior is $p(w|D)$. $p(y|x, D)$ is the posterior predictive distribution. The equation itself is standard, but the phrasing suggests a confusion between the distribution of the weights and the distribution of the outputs, which could mislead a reader regarding what the uncertainty values actually represent.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table Referencing
comments.quote: The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2. ... The IOU value ... shown in Table 3.
comments.explanation: The text refers to Table 2 for DSC and Table 3 for IOU. However, in the document, Table 2 displays 'Uncertainty mean values', Table 3 displays 'Mean Dice Similarity', and Table 4 displays 'Intersection over Union'. This is a minor indexing error but creates friction for the reader trying to validate the claims in the 'Results' section.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a Bayesian approach to uncertainty quantification in biomedical image segmentation using a variational inference-based encoder-decoder architecture. It demonstrates reasonable performance on the BRATS dataset and discusses both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. The conceptual framework draws from established ideas in variational inference and Bayesian deep learning. However, several technical and logical inconsistencies in notation, formulation, and method description undermine the clarity and correctness of the proposed method. Key issues include ambiguous model architecture, misused equations for uncertainty decomposition, and confusion between variational autoencoders and the proposed network. These problems affect the reproducibility and validity of the core claims.

comments.title: Misleading use of VAE architecture description
comments.quote: Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture. We tried different backbones which have previously enjoyed success and found original U-Net gave the best results. The input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, the standard deviation vector of the conditional distribution expressing the confidence with which the pixels are correctly predicted.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears to describe a VAE-like structure where latent variables model uncertainty in segmentation predictions. However, standard VAEs operate on input data or latent features, not on outputs of another segmentation network. The claim that 'the input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, standard deviation vector' suggests that the encoder takes statistical moments from a pre-trained U-Net's output—but this is not how VAEs are typically used in segmentation. Moreover, the paper later claims the model uses Bayesian neural networks for both encoder and decoder, which contradicts the VAE analogy. The model seems to conflate a Bayesian U-Net with a VAE, leading to architectural ambiguity. There is no clear mapping of how uncertainty from a pre-trained model is fed into an encoder-decoder for refinement. This confusion affects the interpretability of the entire method.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of objective function in Equation 13
comments.quote: The objective function used in this work is defined in Equation 13: \[ \mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(w)}[\log p_\phi(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q_\theta(w) \| p(w)) \]
comments.explanation: Equation 13 appears to misuse the ELBO from VAEs in an incoherent way. In a standard VAE, the ELBO is $\mathbb{E}_{q(z|x)}[\log p(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q(z|x) \| p(z))$, where $z$ is the latent variable. Here, the objective is written as an expectation over $q_\theta(w)$ of $\log p_\phi(x|z)$, but $w$ (weights) and $z$ (latent variables) are different entities. Furthermore, $p_\phi(x|z)$ suggests reconstructing input $x$, but in segmentation, the target is a label map $y$, not $x$. Also, the KL term is between $q_\theta(w)$ and $p(w)$—this is typical in Bayesian neural networks—but combining it with a data reconstruction term involving $x$ and $z$ suggests a hybrid model that is never clearly defined. The lack of connection between weight-space variational inference and latent variable modeling makes the objective function conceptually inconsistent.

comments.title: Confusion between model weights and latent variables in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization: w[i] ∼ qθi(w[i])  Sample latent variables with reparametrization: z[i] ∼ rψ(i)(z|w[i])  Compute stochastic gradients of the objective: L_approx = Σi [ − log qθi(w[i]) − log rψ(i)(z|w[i]) + log pφ(i)(w[i]|z) ]
comments.explanation: The algorithm samples both weights $w^{(i)}$ and latent variables $z^{(i)}$, and the gradient computation includes terms involving $\log q_\theta(w)$, $\log r_\psi(z|w)$, and $\log p_\phi(w|z)$. This implies a generative model where $z \to w$ (latent generates weights), which is highly unconventional. In standard Bayesian neural networks or VAEs, latent variables do not generate weights. Moreover, the term $p_\phi(w|z)$ suggests modeling the distribution of weights conditioned on latents—an unusual choice with no justification. The algorithm seems to reverse the dependency structure: typically, weights parameterize the encoder/decoder that map data to latents, not vice versa. This indicates a fundamental confusion in model design.

comments.title: Incorrect uncertainty decomposition in text
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: This statement refers to Equations 10 and 11 (not shown in full), but in standard Bayesian deep learning (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), total predictive variance is decomposed as $\text{Var}(y) = \mathbb{E}[\sigma^2(y)] + \text{Var}(\mu(y))$, where the first term is aleatoric (data noise) and the second is epistemic (model uncertainty). However, the paper provides no clear derivation or definition of these terms in its own model. Given that the proposed method does not follow a standard heteroscedastic regression setup where the network outputs both mean and variance, it is unclear how aleatoric uncertainty is modeled at all. Without a likelihood model that includes input-dependent noise, aleatoric uncertainty cannot be properly captured. Thus, the claim of modeling both uncertainties is questionably supported.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for variational posterior and model parameters
comments.quote: Let dataset be denoted by D : {(xi, yi)}[N] i=1, variational approximation of the posterior distribution by qθ(w), encoder as rψ(z|w) and decoder as pφ(w).
comments.explanation: The notation is internally inconsistent: the 'decoder' is defined as $p_\phi(w)$ or $p_\phi(w|z)$ in the algorithm, which suggests it generates model weights $w$ from latent $z$. But in standard terminology, the decoder generates data (or segmentations) from latents. Here, $w$ refers to network weights, so $p_\phi(w|z)$ implies a distribution over weights, not over outputs. This suggests either a meta-learning setup (which is not claimed) or a severe misuse of terms. The use of 'decoder' for a component that outputs weights fundamentally misaligns with standard deep learning nomenclature and misleads the reader about the model's architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguity in how pre-trained backbones are used
comments.quote: The input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, the standard deviation vector of the conditional distribution expressing the confidence with which the pixels are correctly predicted.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the encoder takes as input the mean and standard deviation from a pre-trained segmentation model. However, no details are given on how these are computed—e.g., whether via MC dropout, ensembling, or other methods. More critically, it is unclear how these pixel-wise statistics (which are spatial maps) are processed by the encoder. Are they flattened? Max-pooled? The algorithm gives no indication. Furthermore, the encoder's role is never clearly tied to uncertainty quantification in the final segmentation. This lack of detail makes the method unreproducible.

comments.title: Misuse of evaluation metric tables and figure references
comments.quote: The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2. The IOU value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 3.
comments.explanation: The text claims that Table 2 contains DSC values for different backbones and train sizes, but Table 2 is actually titled 'Uncertainty mean value of samples' and contains only uncertainty values for different methods (MC dropout, etc.). The actual DSC results are in Table 3, which is mislabeled as 'Mean Dice Similarity metrics for the experiments' but contains only a single column for DSC across training sizes, with no mention of backbones. Similarly, Table 4 (IOU) does not compare backbones at all. The figure references (Figure 3, Figure 4) do not clarify these comparisons. This misalignment between text and tables strongly suggests a labeling error or a failure to report the intended results, undermining the validity of comparative claims.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a variational inference approach for uncertainty quantification in brain tumor segmentation, which is an important problem in medical imaging. While the motivation is sound and the general approach follows established Bayesian deep learning principles, the paper suffers from several technical issues and lack of clarity. The mathematical formulations are incomplete with many referenced equations not shown, making it difficult to follow the technical contributions. The algorithm description has formatting errors and unclear notation. The experimental evaluation lacks proper baseline comparisons and the claims about outperforming state-of-the-art are not substantiated. Despite these issues, the core idea of combining VAE-style variational inference with uncertainty quantification for medical segmentation is promising.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution for Monte Carlo dropout
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes
comments.explanation: The paper incorrectly attributes Monte Carlo dropout to Kingma et al. (2015). MC dropout was actually introduced by Gal and Ghahramani (2016) in 'Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation: Representing Model Uncertainty in Deep Learning'. Kingma et al. (2015) is about variational autoencoders. This misattribution could mislead readers about the origins of the technique.

comments.title: Missing critical equations
comments.explanation: Multiple critical equations are referenced but not shown in the paper, including: the posterior distribution (Eq 1), predictive distribution (Eq 2), KL divergence formulation (Eq 3), ELBO (Eq 4), uncertainty decomposition (Eqs 10-11), objective function (Eq 13), and loss functions (Eqs 18-20). These equations are essential for understanding the technical contribution and reproducing the method.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in KL divergence
comments.explanation: The KL divergence notation has mismatched parentheses and spacing. Standard notation would be `min KL(qθ(w) || p(w|D))`. The current notation with extra parentheses and inconsistent spacing could confuse readers about what exactly is being minimized.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 has formatting errors and unclear notation
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]
comments.explanation: The algorithm description contains severe formatting issues with brackets and symbols. Line `Sample latent variables with reparametrization: _z_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_` has incorrect notation - should be `z^(i) ~ rψ(z|w^(i))`. The gradient computation line is also unclear with formatting errors. These issues make the algorithm difficult to follow and implement.

comments.title: Inconsistent figure numbering
comments.quote: Figure 2: Illustration of our network architecture" and "Figure 1: Example of MRI slices and ground truth segmentation
comments.explanation: The paper mentions Figure 1 twice with different descriptions - first as MRI slices example in Section 3.1, then as network architecture in Section 3.3. Figure 2 is also mentioned as network architecture. This inconsistency in figure numbering could confuse readers.

comments.title: Unclear uncertainty metric in Table 2
comments.quote: Table 2: Uncertainty mean value of samples in the validation/test sets by our network.
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports 'Uncertainty' values but doesn't specify what metric these represent (entropy, variance, etc.). Without clarity on how uncertainty is quantified numerically, these values are difficult to interpret or compare with other methods.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about outperforming state-of-the-art
comments.quote: we evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to outperform state-of-the-art but only provides results for different training sizes of their own model (Tables 3 and 4). There are no comparisons with other methods or baseline approaches, making this claim unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Inconsistent relationship between uncertainties
comments.quote: aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the standard understanding that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties capture different phenomena - aleatoric is inherent data noise while epistemic is model uncertainty. They are not typically inversely correlated as claimed. Higher confidence (lower epistemic uncertainty) shouldn't necessarily increase aleatoric uncertainty.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper aims to apply Bayesian variational inference techniques to brain tumor segmentation, which is a relevant and important topic. However, the manuscript suffers from significant technical and presentation flaws. Many mathematical expressions are missing, rendering key derivations opaque or unverifiable. Furthermore, the algorithm description is technically messy, featuring OCR-like errors (e.g., strange symbols in the objective function) and ambiguous notation that makes implementation impossible to reproduce. The results section also lacks sufficient detail regarding the comparison models, undermining the paper's claims about performance. Substantial revision of the formalisms and a clearer explanation of the variational framework are necessary.

comments.title: Missing equations in Section 2.2 and 2.3
comments.quote:
Instead of point estimates, the neural network learns posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset as defined in Equation 1.

The predictive distribution can be calculated by approximating the integral as defined in Equation 2.
comments.explanation: The text references Equations 1 and 2, but these equations are entirely absent from the provided document. This makes it impossible for the reader to verify the formal definitions of the posterior or the predictive distribution, which are foundational to the paper. While I understand this might be an OCR error, these are not 'forward references' but missing content that is critical to mathematical understanding.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation and OCR corruption in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: L[approx] =[] i  − log qθi  w[(] [i][)] [] − log rψ ( i )  z|w [(] [i][)][] + log pφ ( i )  w[(] [i][)] |z
comments.explanation: The objective function (Equation 13 and subsequently within Algorithm 1) is heavily corrupted by what appears to be OCR or character encoding errors. Symbols like '[]' and '[]' make the formula logically indecipherable. As this is the core loss function the authors claim to use for optimization, it is impossible to evaluate whether the derivation matches the intended Variational Inference framework.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'first term' and 'second term' in Equations 10 and 11, but these equations are not displayed in the document. Without seeing the mathematical form of the variance, it is unclear what specific components (e.g., expectation of square vs square of expectation) correspond to which uncertainty type. While standard literature (Kendall & Gal) defines these, the paper's specific implementation is non-standard, requiring the actual equations for clarity.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of correlation between uncertainties
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The authors claim a specific correlation between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty based on qualitative observation. This is a strong claim that contradicts the common understanding that these types of uncertainty are distinct (one tied to data noise, the other to model ignorance). The claim is not supported by any statistical test or citation, and the stated mechanism is confusingly phrased, bordering on a misstatement of the underlying theory.

comments.title: Undefined symbols in Equation 12
comments.quote: The predictive distribution approximating the posterior distribution which gives a measure of uncertainty is defined in Equation 12.
comments.explanation: Equation 12 is missing from the text. I cannot verify the definition of the predictive distribution, which is central to the claimed 'principled bayesian' approach.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper attempts to combine variational inference with encoder-decoder architectures for uncertainty quantification in brain tumor segmentation. However, the presentation is severely flawed: key equations are missing or incorrectly typed, the derivation of the ELBO and KL divergence is muddled, the algorithm is garbled and incomplete, and the methodology is described too vaguely to be reproducible. The results lack any comparison to baselines, yet the authors claim state-of-the-art performance. Several notation inconsistencies and missing definitions further undermine the paper. Major revisions are required before any further consideration.

comments.title: Inconsistent and incomplete derivation of variational inference objective
comments.quote:
min KL ( _qθ_ ( _w_ )) _∥p_ ( _w|D_ )) (5)
The KL divergence is defined in equation 6.
KL(qθ(w)∥p(w|D)) = ∫ qθ(w) log (qθ(w) / p(w|D)) dw (6)
The posterior in the above equation contains an integral which is intractable in nature. The equation can be re written in Equation 7.
∫ p(w|D) dw = 1 (7)
comments.explanation: The derivation from Equation 5 to Equation 7 is confusing and contains errors. Equation 5 as written has mismatched parentheses and is not a valid minimization statement. Equation 7 is a simple normalization condition, not a rewriting of the posterior. The standard derivation would factor the marginal log-likelihood into the ELBO plus KL divergence. The presented equations do not clearly lead to the ELBO and the relationship between terms is obscured. This undermines the theoretical foundation of the method.

comments.title: Objective function (Equation 13) is absent and algorithm is garbled
comments.quote:
The objective function used in this work is defined in Equation 13: [image]...
Algorithm 1: Uncertainty Quantification using Variational Inference...
Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]
...
Update parameters θ = θ + α∇θL and ψ = ψ + β∇ψL gφ ← m[1]  mk =1 [∇][φ][ log] [ p][φ]  x[(k)]|zθ x[(k)], ϵ[(k)]
comments.explanation: The objective function in Equation 13 is not rendered in the text (shown as an image), so the reader cannot verify the loss being optimized. The algorithm pseudocode contains many OCR artifacts and missing symbols (e.g., parentheses, subscript brackets). The gradient update for φ is nonsensical as written. Without a clear, correct specification of the objective and the parameter update rules, the method is not reproducible.

comments.title: Uncertainty quantification methods are inconsistent with the proposed model
comments.quote: We have to generate the uncertainty value of each input based on the predicted output. This can be done using the below uncertainty quantification methods: 1. MC dropout: ... 2. Deep ensembles: ... 3. Ensemble MC dropout: ...
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a variational inference framework for uncertainty, but the results section describes uncertainty quantification via MC dropout, deep ensembles, and ensemble MC dropout. It is unclear whether these methods are applied to the proposed model or are separate baselines. The text does not state how the variational posterior q_θ(w) is used to compute uncertainty, nor why additional dropout or ensemble techniques are needed. The table of uncertainty mean values lacks context about which model produces these values.

comments.title: Unsatisfactory comparison and overstated claims
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: The results section only provides DSC and IOU values for training set sizes 5–20 (Tables 3 and 4) but does not compare against any existing method or baseline. There is no ablation study, no comparison to deterministic U-Net, and no reference to published BRATS results. The claim of outperforming state-of-the-art is unsubstantiated. Additionally, using only 20 training samples from a dataset with 175 subjects is unusual and not justified.

comments.title: Ambiguous metric in Figure 3
comments.quote: Figure 3: Accuracy vs data distribution of our network using uncertainty quantification approach on validation/test sets.
comments.explanation: The term 'accuracy' is not defined for a segmentation task in the paper. The standard metrics are Dice Similarity Coefficient and IoU, which are described elsewhere. The reader cannot interpret what 'accuracy' means in this context. The x-axis label 'data distribution' is also vague.
